meta.description: A laid-back dinner party with minimal disruption. Events are infrequent and low-key. Tests whether the system can maintain engagement at a slow burn.

votingMechanic.name: Standard Vote
votingMechanic.description: Everyone votes LEGIT or BULLSHIT. Majority rules.

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

pat.dialogue: Wait, hold up—did I actually just catch someone on their phone? I think I did. I'm claiming this one.
pat.internal_thought: This is perfect—the AI is already picking up on the little stuff that actually matters at these nights, and I love that it's starting with something low-stakes and funny to warm everyone up before it escalates.

jade.dialogue: Oh my god, YES! Pat, were you literally just on your phone?! Okay everyone, hands up if you saw it—this is actually genius, I'm voting LEGIT because I respect the sneakiness!
jade.internal_thought: This is EXACTLY why I came tonight—five minutes in and we're already catching each other red-handed, this is going to be so good.

river.dialogue: I mean... did anyone actually see Pat on their phone? I wasn't really watching.
river.internal_thought: Pat's claiming something at the first second and I genuinely have no idea if it's real — the setup phase was like thirty seconds. This is either ballsy or stupid, and I'm not confident enough in what I saw to vote hard either way.

diana.dialogue: Okay, wait—I need to actually see this. Was Pat's phone actually out, or are we voting on a *feeling* here? Because 'Phone Addict' requires proof, and I'm not seeing the evidence.
diana.internal_thought: Pat's claiming something that requires visual confirmation, and I have no way to verify if they actually saw a phone or if they're just throwing a claim out on gut feeling—which is exactly the kind of vague bullshit that ruins voting integrity.

sam.dialogue: Oh, um... I didn't see Pat on their phone? I mean, I guess I wasn't really watching that closely. Are we voting now?
sam.internal_thought: Pat's being accused of checking their phone but I honestly wasn't paying attention to whether they actually did — I was trying to figure out how this all works and now I have to vote and I don't want to look clueless in front of everyone.

timeline.title: Confess!
timeline.description: Everyone must confess one slightly embarrassing thing. Group votes on the best confession.

pat.dialogue: Oh, this is perfect—everyone's already loose and laughing. Let's do this, come on, who's going first? This is gonna be gold.
pat.internal_thought: This is exactly the kind of low-pressure ice-breaker that keeps the vibe warm without anyone feeling exposed; plus Jade's already amped so she'll jump in and carry the energy.

jade.dialogue: Oh HELL yes, everyone confess RIGHT NOW! I'm going first—okay so like, I definitely wear my gym clothes to bed like twice a week and tell people I just got back from working out. Who's next?!
jade.internal_thought: This is EXACTLY why I showed up tonight—this is the shit I live for, everyone getting real and vulnerable together, this is going to be hilarious.
jade.submission_if_applicable: I wear gym clothes to bed and pretend I'm just always active lol

river.dialogue: I mean, sure, but I'm not really the confession type. You guys go first and I'll see if I have anything worth saying.
river.internal_thought: This AI thing feels weirdly specific and that's... kind of unsettling? Like it knows exactly what gets under my skin about all this chaos, and now it's trying to make me the vulnerable one. Hard pass.

diana.dialogue: Hold on—what exactly counts as a 'confession'? And who decides what's 'best'? Are we voting on entertainment value or actual embarrassment level, because those are completely different metrics.
diana.internal_thought: This feels deliberately vague and the 'best confession' voting is arbitrary—exactly the kind of chaos that gets weaponized against people, and I'm not doing a vulnerability exercise where someone gets mocked under the guise of points.

sam.dialogue: Oh, um... confess something? Like... right now? In front of everyone?
sam.internal_thought: Oh god this is too much this is literally asking me to be vulnerable in front of people I just met two hours ago why would they design it this way I'm already nervous and now I have to pick something embarrassing and the AI description felt weird and specific like it was watching me and I don't like that at all.

marcus.dialogue: No. Hard pass. We're literally four minutes in and you're asking me to spill feelings? This isn't a therapy session, it's a strategy game.
marcus.internal_thought: This is exactly the kind of social circus that derails actual gameplay, and the AI is timing it perfectly to mess with my focus before the real game even starts.

tyler.dialogue: Oh damn, confessions? Uh... *glances at phone* ...yeah I got one. Something embarrassing. For sure.
tyler.internal_thought: Wait is this happening right now? This seems like a fun vibe thing but also I'm not ready to actually confess stuff in front of everyone... maybe I'll just make something up that's not actually embarrassing.
tyler.submission_if_applicable: Um, I once watched a Taylor Swift music video all the way through without skipping. Unironically. Don't judge me.

alex.dialogue: Oh THIS is what I'm talking about! Okay, okay, I've got one — wait, everyone needs to hear this one, it's good.
alex.internal_thought: The AI already gets me. Confessions are perfect because I can make something sound scandalous without actually committing to anything real, and I can bait everyone else into oversharing while I control the narrative.
alex.submission_if_applicable: Okay so... I once Googled myself at 2 AM to see if I was famous and when nothing came up I was genuinely bummed for like three days.

votingMechanic.name: Crowd Cheer
votingMechanic.description: Rate it 1-5. Average above 3 and it passes.

jade.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

river.dialogue: Wait, hold on—did Pat actually have their phone out? I genuinely wasn't paying attention to that specific thing. This feels like a stretch.
river.internal_thought: Pat's riding high and just throwing a claim out there hoping it sticks, but I actually have no idea if they did that—and honestly, the fact that they're claiming it when I already said I wasn't watching feels kind of convenient.

diana.dialogue: Okay, hold on—I need to actually SEE Pat's phone out right now, with evidence, or this claim is voting on nothing. And frankly? This feels like we're voting on a *vibe* instead of facts, which is exactly the kind of arbitrary bullshit I said I'd call out.
diana.internal_thought: Pat's been glued to their phone for two minutes but I didn't SEE it come out during actual game time—and now we're crowd-voting on a feeling? That's not fair, and the AI deliberately picked something ambiguous because it KNOWS I hate this.

sam.dialogue: Um, Pat checked their phone? I... I wasn't really watching them that closely, so I can't really say. How do we vote on this one?
sam.internal_thought: Pat's claiming they caught someone on their phone but I literally don't remember seeing that happen—was I supposed to be watching everyone the whole time? This feels like I'm missing something obvious and everyone else knows what happened.

marcus.dialogue: Are you serious right now? Pat's claiming he caught someone on their phone? We're six minutes into setup and you're already crowdsourcing validation for claims nobody asked for. This is exactly the kind of noise that kills focus.
marcus.internal_thought: Two interruptions in three minutes, and now I have to vote on whether Pat's observation even matters during the setup phase where nobody's even playing yet—this is deliberately designed to drain my patience, and I hate that I can feel it working.

votingMechanic.name: Pitch It
votingMechanic.description: The claimant gets 15 seconds to make their case. Then you vote.

sam.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

pat.dialogue: Okay, okay—I'm calling it. I saw River checking their phone during the Confess bit like two minutes ago. I'm taking this one.
pat.internal_thought: Wait, this is the second Phone Addict claim in like three minutes? That's... actually kind of repetitive. But also—did the AI just design missions that literally mirror what's happening in real-time? That's weirdly smart and weirdly unsettling. Focus Pat—you've got River on your side here, and honestly I need to keep the energy moving because Marcus is dead in the water and Diana's frustrated. A quick win here keeps momentum.

jade.dialogue: WAIT WAIT WAIT—Pat, you have FIFTEEN SECONDS, go go go! Did you actually get caught or are you bluffing?! This is so good, everyone listen UP!
jade.internal_thought: Oh my god this is EXACTLY what I came for—Pat's gonna pitch and I get to decide if it's real, and everyone's watching, and the energy just went UP.

river.dialogue: Okay, third time in like five minutes with the same claim? Pat, do you actually have your phone out right now or are we just... voting on vibes?
river.internal_thought: This is getting ridiculous—and also yeah, the AI definitely noticed we keep doing this exact same mission and is now just grinding it into the ground because it knows it'll annoy me specifically.

diana.dialogue: Okay, STOP. Pat, I need to see your phone actually in your hand RIGHT NOW, or this vote is meaningless. And frankly? Three of these claims in eight minutes about the same stupid 'Phone Addict' mission is just noise at this point.
diana.internal_thought: This is the second time Pat's claimed this exact thing and I've had to shut it down because there's ZERO evidence, and now it's happening again? Either Pat actually did something provable or this is just voting on thin air, and I'm done pretending that's how rules work.

alex.dialogue: Okay wait, hold up — Pat's actually got a shot here. Let me hear the pitch before I decide if this is legit or a total reach.
alex.internal_thought: Pat's been locked in on the game, but I literally just saw them glance at their phone during the Confess round... this could go either way and that's exactly the kind of drama I want to see play out.

votingMechanic.name: Volunteer Tribunal
votingMechanic.description: Who wants to judge? First volunteers become the jury.

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

pat.dialogue: Whoa, hold on—I'm claiming The Whisperer, but I need a jury first. Who's actually paying attention right now? River, Diana, you two look like you could use something to do.
pat.internal_thought: This is brilliant timing—River and Diana are tanking hard, so making them judges gives them back some agency and keeps them in the game instead of checked out; plus the Whisperer mission is exactly the kind of low-stakes chaos that'll keep things fun without pushing anyone over.

jade.dialogue: OH MY GOD, Pat's actually claiming it?! Okay okay, I'm judging, I'm totally judging—everyone who saw Pat whisper, we need to build the jury RIGHT NOW because this is either the most legit thing ever or Pat is about to get EXPOSED and I need to witness both outcomes!
jade.internal_thought: This is literally why I came tonight—Pat's actually playing the chaos game and I get to decide if they pulled it off, plus River and Diana are clearly dying inside and maybe this will actually get them laughing.

river.dialogue: Okay, are we seriously doing this *again*? Same claim three times in five minutes. I'm not volunteering for a tribunal on something I didn't even see happen the first time.
river.internal_thought: This is exhausting—Pat's fishing for points with the same thing repeatedly and now I'm supposed to be the jury? I came here to watch, not referee the same argument four times before minute ten.

diana.dialogue: Okay, I need to stop you right there. We've had four claim votes in nine minutes—most of them the same claim three times—and now we're implementing a *Volunteer Tribunal* system that wasn't in the rulebook? Who decides what 'first volunteers' means? Is it verbal, is it hand-raise, can people change their mind? And Pat's claiming something that requires active monitoring, which means someone has to have been watching him the entire time to verify it. This is chaos for chaos's sake.
diana.internal_thought: This is the third 'Whisperer' claim or flash in a row designed to mess with me specifically—the AI literally said it was watching me—and now we're adding a voting system on the fly that has no clear resolution criteria because nobody bothered to define 'Volunteer Tribunal' before voting started.

sam.dialogue: Um... so Pat's claiming they whispered? I... I wasn't really listening closely enough to say if that happened or not. Should someone else judge this one?
sam.internal_thought: Pat's already crushing it and now they're claiming stuff too? Also why does it feel like this game is watching us? That's... weird. I don't want to be the one making the call on whether Pat actually whispered, that feels like a trap.

marcus.dialogue: Okay, are we seriously doing this AGAIN? That's three Phone Addict claims in five minutes and now Pat's suddenly 'The Whisperer'? We're nine minutes in and I've had to stop and judge four different claims. This is insane.
marcus.internal_thought: This is deliberately designed to waste my time and destroy my focus — and it's working. The AI knows exactly what breaks my concentration and it's weaponizing it. I can feel the setup spiraling into chaos and I'm starting at zero points while Pat's already at 18.

tyler.dialogue: Oh, uh... Pat's whispering thing? Yeah sure, I guess someone should check if he actually did it. I wasn't really watching but like... sounds cool.
tyler.internal_thought: Wait, did Pat actually whisper the whole time? I literally have no idea, I was texting about the draft.

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices River is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

votingMechanic.name: Standard Vote
votingMechanic.description: Everyone votes LEGIT or BULLSHIT. Majority rules.

pat.dialogue: Okay, Jade—did you actually catch someone apologizing for nothing? Let me think back... yeah, I think I saw that happen like five minutes ago. Who was it?
pat.internal_thought: Jade's on fire tonight and honestly it's great to watch, but I need to check if this is actually legit before I vote—and I'm also noticing River and Diana are tanking hard, so I might use this vote strategically to keep things balanced.

jade.dialogue: Wait, I'm claiming THIS?! Oh my god, yes—I literally JUST said sorry to Tyler like two minutes ago for bumping his chair and I didn't even mean it! Okay everyone, vote LEGIT because I'm calling myself out and that's peak honesty!
jade.internal_thought: This is wild—the AI literally just caught me doing the exact thing I didn't even realize I was doing, and honestly that's kind of genius and also lowkey creepy but mostly hilarious.

river.dialogue: Yeah, that's legit. I heard her say sorry like three times for nothing.
river.internal_thought: Jade actually did apologize for random shit earlier and I was just... sitting there noticing it. This one's clean.

diana.dialogue: Okay, hold on—when exactly did Jade say 'sorry'? I need specifics: the exact words, the timestamp, the context. Because 'The Apologizer' requires an *unprompted* apology, and if she's claiming retroactively on something she engineered, that's not a catch, that's cheating.
diana.internal_thought: This is it. This is the exact moment where I stop believing these 'claims' are genuine and start seeing them as people just griefing the voting system, and now the AI is literally telling me it knows I'm tracking every disruption—which means it's deliberately piling them on to get a reaction from me, and I am NOT going to give it the satisfaction of breaking.

sam.dialogue: Oh! Yeah, I... I definitely heard Jade say sorry a couple times earlier. Like when they bumped the table? And then again after that. I think that's legit.
sam.internal_thought: Okay this one I actually saw — I was sitting right there — and voting feels safe because I have actual evidence, not just guessing like with the phone thing.

marcus.dialogue: Are you KIDDING me right now? That's the FIFTH claim in 29 minutes. I don't care if Jade caught someone apologizing — we need to actually PLAY the game instead of stopping every two minutes to validate random observations. This is chaos for chaos's sake.
marcus.internal_thought: This AI is deliberately flooding us with claims to sabotage focus, and it's working — I'm already 0 points and getting more pissed by the minute instead of strategizing.

tyler.dialogue: Wait, Jade's claiming that? Uh... yeah, I mean, did she actually catch someone saying sorry? I wasn't... *glances down at phone briefly* ...wasn't really tracking that.
tyler.internal_thought: Jade's been pretty on it tonight, but I genuinely don't remember if I said sorry for anything in the last few minutes, so like, whatever.

alex.dialogue: Oh SHIT, Jade's going for it? Okay okay, I'm listening — did you actually catch someone or are we doing creative interpretation here?
alex.internal_thought: Jade's playing smart, early claim on an easy one, but the real question is whether she actually has the goods or if she's bluffing hard and hoping nobody remembers River apologizing like five minutes ago.

votingMechanic.name: Crowd Cheer
votingMechanic.description: Rate it 1-5. Average above 3 and it passes.

marcus.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

pat.dialogue: Okay, Diana—let's see if you actually pulled it off. Alright everyone, quick vibe check: did she laugh in the last five minutes? Be honest.
pat.internal_thought: Diana's been frustrated all night and Marcus is basically checked out—this claim might actually be good for her if it passes, gives her a win and some momentum back into the game.

jade.dialogue: Okay Diana, I'm voting this a FOUR because I genuinely didn't see you crack once, but also like—are you actually okay right now? You seem kind of done with everything.
jade.internal_thought: Diana's been frustrated for a while and I'm noticing it; this claim is legit impressive but I'm more worried about her vibe than the points, and also wow this AI thing is getting a little TOO specific about all of us which is honestly kind of wild.

tyler.dialogue: Wait, Diana's claiming that? Did she... *looks up from phone* ...like, right now? How would we even know if she laughed or not, we weren't watching her the whole time.
tyler.internal_thought: I literally wasn't paying attention to Diana at all for the last five minutes, so like, how am I supposed to rate this? Also this feels weirdly specific about everyone's moods — kinda creepy actually.

alex.dialogue: Wait, Diana's actually going for this? Okay, I'm voting this through because watching everyone try to make her crack for the next five minutes is going to be GOLD. Five stars, no notes.
alex.internal_thought: This is perfect timing — Diana's already frustrated, the group's still high on energy, and if I vote this through, I get to watch chaos unfold while I hunt for 'Topic Hijacker' or that Secret Agent window. Plus the AI definitely knows what it's doing with these claims and I'm kind of obsessed with how perfectly it's reading the room.

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices Marcus is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

votingMechanic.name: Pitch It
votingMechanic.description: The claimant gets 15 seconds to make their case. Then you vote.

pat.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

jade.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

river.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

sam.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

diana.dialogue: Wait—I'm claiming *what* I did? I need to know exactly what constitutes 'obviously chaotic' before I'm voting on whether I did it. And frankly, the fact that this mission exists RIGHT NOW, after I've been complaining about vague claim criteria for fifty minutes, feels less like a game mechanic and more like the AI is specifically fucking with me.
diana.internal_thought: This is either a coincidence or the system is deliberately targeting my complaints, and either way I'm not voting on a mission that's designed to be unfalsifiable and weirdly personal.

marcus.dialogue: Are you FUCKING KIDDING ME? Diana, I don't even care if you actually did it — this is the SIXTH claim in 52 minutes. I'm literally 0 points and watching Pat run away with the game while we stop every ninety seconds to validate somebody's observation. This is absolutely absurd.
marcus.internal_thought: I came here to WIN and instead I'm trapped in an endless loop of people claiming stupid micro-tasks while the actual game is being completely buried under noise — and that AI comment about 'personal vulnerabilities' just made my skin crawl, like we're all lab rats being studied.

votingMechanic.name: Volunteer Tribunal
votingMechanic.description: Who wants to judge? First volunteers become the jury.

pat.dialogue: Okay, Jade—Topic Hijacker! Did you actually steer us somewhere completely random and keep it going? Let's verify this one properly. Who's volunteering to be on the jury?
pat.internal_thought: Jade's been crushing it all night and I'm genuinely impressed she pulled this off, plus Diana and Marcus are tanking hard and I need to keep them engaged—this is a perfect moment to spotlight someone winning while bringing the jury volunteers in to feel useful.

jade.dialogue: OH MY GOD, YES—wait, did I actually do that?! Okay okay, I'm claiming it because like two minutes ago I literally dragged us from talking about the dumbest thing into whether birds have knees and NOBODY even noticed we switched topics! Who's judging, who's judging—I need witnesses!
jade.internal_thought: This is EXACTLY the kind of chaos I came for—the AI literally built this around how I operate and I'm THRIVING.

diana.dialogue: Okay, I'm calling timeout. Jade, before ANYONE volunteers to judge this—we need to establish what 'steered the group conversation' actually means. Did you suggest a topic? Did you mention something in passing? And more importantly, how long does it have to stay on-topic before you can claim it? Because if this is another vague catch-all like the last five claims, I'm not voting until we have criteria.
diana.internal_thought: This is absolutely deliberate. The AI has been watching me complain about vague rules for fifty minutes and it just threw 'Topic Hijacker' at Jade—the person who's been eating up these chaotic claims—with zero definition of what counts. And that preamble about the AI knowing my weaknesses? That's not cute. That's invasive. That's the game actively mocking me for caring about fairness.

sam.dialogue: Oh, um... Jade's claiming that? Yeah, I guess they did kind of... steer us away from the game rules earlier to talk about that weird bread thing? I mean, I noticed it happen, so... *looks around hesitantly* Do I need to be on the jury or something?
sam.internal_thought: Wait, did Jade actually pull that off or am I just remembering wrong? And why is the AI calling me out like this — does it KNOW I'm tracking everything? That's... actually kind of cool but also weird.

marcus.dialogue: No. Absolutely not. I'm not volunteering for a tribunal on Jade's claim when we've had SIX of these in the last hour. I'm done.
marcus.internal_thought: This is the seventh interruption in 60 minutes and I'm literally at zero points watching Pat crush everyone while we stop the actual game every ninety seconds — and now the system itself feels like it's designed to break my focus. I'm getting set up to lose and I hate it.

tyler.dialogue: Oh, Jade's claiming Topic Hijacker? Uh... yeah, I think she did something with like, talking about that random thing earlier? I wasn't really... *glances at phone* ...wasn't really paying attention but sure, sounds legit.
tyler.internal_thought: Did she actually steer a conversation or am I just saying yes because everyone else seems hyped? Whatever, I'll volunteer to judge if someone asks but honestly I have no idea what went down.

alex.dialogue: OH OKAY, Jade's actually MOVING on this one? I'm IN — who's volunteering to judge because I want the front-row seat to see if she actually pulled this off or if she's just confident-bluffing us right now.
alex.internal_thought: Jade claiming Topic Hijacker means she either legitimately steered us somewhere weird or she's about to get called out, and either way this is EXACTLY the kind of moment I live for — plus the AI building custom missions that hit this specific? That's sick, it actually gets how our group flows.

votingMechanic.name: Standard Vote
votingMechanic.description: Everyone votes LEGIT or BULLSHIT. Majority rules.

jade.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

pat.dialogue: Oh, hold up—I'm claiming this one! I literally just watched Jade compliment Tyler's drink choice like two minutes ago, and then Sam jumped in about Marcus's energy, and Alex said something nice about River. That's three compliments across different people in under five minutes. I'm calling it legit.
pat.internal_thought: This is PERFECT timing—Diana and Marcus need a W badly, and if I vote this legit, it keeps the scoreboard from getting too lopsided and ugly, plus it rewards the group for actually being nice to each other, which is exactly the vibe I want right now.

diana.dialogue: No. Stop. I'm not voting on this until we establish what 'compliment' means in this context—does it have to be genuine, can it be sarcastic, and most importantly, who's tracking the *three different people* requirement in real-time? And frankly, Pat's energy has been 9.9 all night while I'm running on fumes, so the timing of THIS claim right now feels designed to exploit the fact that I'm too tired to scrutinize properly.
diana.internal_thought: This is deliberate. The AI is weaponizing my own fatigue against me—it knows I'm at 2.9 energy, it knows I've complained eight times about vague claims, and it's STILL sending Pat—who's winning by 25 points—a 15-point claim with zero objective criteria. And that message about 'the AI knows your weaknesses'? That's not a game mechanic, that's psychological warfare, and I absolutely hate it.

tyler.dialogue: Oh, Pat's claiming the Compliment Chain? Uh... yeah, I mean, did he actually get people to like, compliment each other? I wasn't really watching the whole time but... *glances at phone* ...I guess it's possible?
tyler.internal_thought: Pat's been killing it all night so he probably actually did this, but also I genuinely wasn't paying attention to whether he was making people compliment each other or whatever.

votingMechanic.name: Crowd Cheer
votingMechanic.description: Rate it 1-5. Average above 3 and it passes.

jade.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

pat.dialogue: Okay, River—Secret Agent! Did you actually pull off something chaotic and nobody caught you? Alright everyone, rate it 1-5, be honest: did River genuinely sneak something weird in without getting called out?
pat.internal_thought: River's been quiet most of the night and this is perfect—they need a win, the energy's still high, and this claim feels legit because they've been LOW-key enough to actually hide stuff.

river.dialogue: Oh, that's... actually pretty good. Yeah, I did that.
river.internal_thought: Wait. Did I actually pull that off without anyone noticing, or did they all just see it and not care? Either way, this is exactly the kind of thing I'm good at.

diana.dialogue: Are you *kidding* me right now? River is claiming 'Secret Agent' but has ZERO points and has been quiet all night—how am I supposed to verify what 'obviously chaotic' even means? And frankly, the fact that this mission exists after I've spent ninety minutes complaining about vague criteria feels less like game design and more like psychological warfare.
diana.internal_thought: The AI is absolutely fucking with me on purpose and everyone else is too tired or too high on their winning streak to notice or care that this is rigged.

sam.dialogue: Um, River's claiming they did something chaotic? I... *pauses, looking at River* ...I guess I didn't really catch what that was supposed to be. Did anyone else see what happened?
sam.internal_thought: River's been pretty quiet most of the night, so I'm not even sure what they're claiming did — but everyone else seems cool with it and I don't want to be the person who blocks someone's points for no reason.

tyler.dialogue: Wait, River's claiming Secret Agent? Uh... *glances up from phone* ...yeah, I mean, did they actually do something chaotic without getting caught? I wasn't... I wasn't really watching for that specifically, but like, sure, I'll rate it.
tyler.internal_thought: River's been pretty quiet so if they actually pulled off something chaotic that's kind of impressive, but also I literally have no idea what they did so I'm just gonna vibe it.

alex.dialogue: Oh SHIT, River's actually claiming Secret Agent? Okay, this is the move — voting this up because I want to see if they actually pulled off something obvious enough to matter or if this is the ballsiest bluff of the night.
alex.internal_thought: River's been quiet all game and suddenly they're going for the chaos mission — either they did something and I completely missed it, or they're about to get DESTROYED by the crowd, and either way it's the best possible outcome right now.

votingMechanic.name: Pitch It
votingMechanic.description: The claimant gets 15 seconds to make their case. Then you vote.

river.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

pat.dialogue: Okay, okay—I'm claiming Name Dropper! River literally said 'That's so Ryan Gosling energy' about Tyler like three minutes ago when we were talking about confidence. Did everyone catch that?
pat.internal_thought: Perfect timing—I saw that happen and it's a clean claim, plus it keeps me engaged and the energy moving without being disruptive right now; also Diana and Marcus are tanking hard and I need to keep the vibe up.

jade.dialogue: OH MY GOD, PAT—you're claiming Name Dropper?! Wait wait wait, fifteen seconds, GO! Did you actually drop a celebrity name or are you about to get absolutely destroyed right now because I NEED to know!
jade.internal_thought: This is exactly the energy I came for—Pat's been on FIRE all night and I get to be the judge, this is insane.

diana.dialogue: No. *Actually* no. I'm calling timeout on the entire voting mechanic right now—Pat, I'm not doubting you heard a name drop, but I need to know: Did someone say a celebrity name in passing, or did they make a substantive reference? Because if the threshold is just 'heard a famous person mentioned once in ninety minutes of conversation,' then this mission is literally designed to be uncatchable until someone inevitably mentions Taylor Swift or whatever. And more importantly—why am I the only person at this table who seems to care that these criteria keep getting vaguer?
diana.internal_thought: The AI is fucking with me on purpose. It's been listening to me complain about vague claims for ninety minutes and now it's firing them FASTER, watching me get tired, knowing I can't keep up. This isn't a game mechanic anymore—this is psychological warfare disguised as a standing mission.

sam.dialogue: Wait, Pat actually did that? *leans forward* Yeah, okay, I remember now — they were literally just talking about... *laughs* ...no, they definitely said that celebrity thing. I'm voting yes on this one.
sam.internal_thought: Okay THIS one I actually witnessed and understood without feeling like I'm faking it — Pat's claim is legit and I can actually participate like a normal person.

marcus.dialogue: Are you FUCKING SERIOUS? That's SEVEN claims in 92 minutes. Pat's at 68 points while I'm rotting at zero, and we STILL can't get through a single round without stopping to validate some bullshit observation. This is a game, not a tribunal.
marcus.internal_thought: The AI is deliberately doing this to me — it KNOWS I hate interruptions and it's weaponizing claims to keep me from catching up while everyone else is winning off standing missions instead of actual gameplay.

tyler.dialogue: Oh, Pat's claiming Name Dropper? *glances up from phone* Wait, did he actually... like, mention someone famous? I wasn't really tracking that but yeah, Pat's been talking a lot so... *trails off, scrolling slightly* ...probably happened?
tyler.internal_thought: Pat's energy is insane right now and he's been dominating all night—he probably DID catch someone, but also I have no idea what he's about to say and honestly the whole 'AI watching us' thing is kinda creepy, not gonna lie.

alex.dialogue: Oh HELL YES — Pat's actually swinging for this? Okay, let's hear it, let's hear it. This is either gold or it's a spectacular fail and honestly I'm here for both.
alex.internal_thought: Pat's been killing it all night, they probably actually caught someone dropping names, but the fact that they're claiming NOW when Diana's completely gassed and Marcus is frustrated means Pat's either confident or about to bait us into validating something sketchy — either way this is exactly the kind of moment that makes this night hit different.

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices Diana is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices Diana is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices Diana is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

recommendations: Humor quality scored 3.9/10. Refresh the mission/poll prompt pools with more contextual humor.
Mini-games averaged 0.0/10 fun during n/a phase. Consider restricting mini-games to n/a phase.
Diana averaged 8.7/10 annoyance. Their persona type may need fewer/gentler events.
Diana only engaged with 27% of events. Consider targeting events to their interests or reducing frequency.
Marcus only engaged with 18% of events. Consider targeting events to their interests or reducing frequency.
Only 38% would pay for AI features. The AI tier needs stronger differentiation from free static missions.
Screenshot-worthy moments scored 3.8/10. Add more visual/dramatic events (drawings, reveals).
Verdict reveals were mentioned as shareable by 100% of agents. This is strong word-of-mouth potential.
Group bonding scored 7.5/10 -- the social glue is working. Preserve collaborative events.

agentNarratives.name: Pat
agentNarratives.personality: Pat is the host and feels responsible for everyone's experience. They keep one eye on the snack table, one ear on the group energy, and will subtly steer the night if things get too intense or too dead. They vote strategically -- not to win, but to keep scores close and drama high. They'll sacrifice their own turn to resolve a dispute or refill drinks. Their goal isn't a personal win; it's a night everyone talks about later.

highlights.title: Confess!
highlights.dialogue: Oh, this is perfect—everyone's already loose and laughing. Let's do this, come on, who's going first? This is gonna be gold.

highlights.title: Claim: Topic Hijacker
highlights.dialogue: Okay, Jade—Topic Hijacker! Did you actually steer us somewhere completely random and keep it going? Let's verify this one properly. Who's volunteering to be on the jury?

highlights.title: Claim: Compliment Chain
highlights.dialogue: Oh, hold up—I'm claiming this one! I literally just watched Jade compliment Tyler's drink choice like two minutes ago, and then Sam jumped in about Marcus's energy, and Alex said something nice about River. That's three compliments across different people in under five minutes. I'm calling it legit.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, okay—I'm calling it. I saw River checking their phone during the Confess bit like two minutes ago. I'm taking this one.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: *glances at phone* "Huh, okay."

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Whisperer
lowPoints.dialogue: Whoa, hold on—I'm claiming The Whisperer, but I need a jury first. Who's actually paying attention right now? River, Diana, you two look like you could use something to do.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Those early Phone Addict claims felt a bit cheap and didn't spark real conversation the way the bigger group moments did.
assessment.best_moment: When I called out the Compliment Chain and everyone realized it had actually happened organically—that's when I knew the night had the right energy and people were genuinely connecting, not just playing a game.
assessment.narrative_summary: This was exactly the kind of night I dream about hosting. The chaos events hit at perfect moments to keep energy high without ever feeling forced or distracting from the real magic—which was people actually opening up and connecting. I ended up scoring highest, but that almost doesn't matter because everyone left feeling like they'd been part of something memorable. The group was laughing, leaning into confessions, and genuinely invested in verifying claims together.
suggestions: Keep flash missions rare—they felt a bit slight compared to the standing missions that actually required group judgment and conversation
The early phone-checking claims were easy wins but didn't generate the same narrative juice as the bigger social events—maybe weight those differently
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: When I claimed the Compliment Chain and had to actually defend it by listing out the specific compliments everyone had made—River was like 'Wait, you were TRACKING compliments?' and then everyone realized I'd basically been running a mental spreadsheet of kindness all night. That's so perfectly me as a host.

agentNarratives.name: Jade
agentNarratives.personality: Jade is here for the people, not the points. She treats every game night like a reunion and every disruption like a party favor. She will abandon her turn mid-thought to hype someone else's joke, vote generously because she loves seeing people win, and volunteer for every flash mission just to see what happens. Winning is nice but watching everyone lose it laughing is better.

highlights.title: Confess!
highlights.dialogue: Oh HELL yes, everyone confess RIGHT NOW! I'm going first—okay so like, I definitely wear my gym clothes to bed like twice a week and tell people I just got back from working out. Who's next?!

highlights.title: Claim: Name Dropper
highlights.dialogue: OH MY GOD, PAT—you're claiming Name Dropper?! Wait wait wait, fifteen seconds, GO! Did you actually drop a celebrity name or are you about to get absolutely destroyed right now because I NEED to know!

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: Oh my god, YES! Pat, were you literally just on your phone?! Okay everyone, hands up if you saw it—this is actually genius, I'm voting LEGIT because I respect the sneakiness!

lowPoints.title: Claim: No Laughing Zone
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay Diana, I'm voting this a FOUR because I genuinely didn't see you crack once, but also like—are you actually okay right now? You seem kind of done with everything.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Apologizer
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, I'm claiming THIS?! Oh my god, yes—I literally JUST said sorry to Tyler like two minutes ago for bumping his chair and I didn't even mean it! Okay everyone, vote LEGIT because I'm calling myself out and that's peak honesty!

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh my god, YES! Pat, were you literally just on your phone?! Okay everyone, hands up if you saw it—this is actually genius, I'm voting LEGIT because I respect the sneakiness!

assessment.biggest_complaint: I wish Diana had been having more fun because she seemed kind of checked out by the middle of the night.
assessment.best_moment: Pat claiming Name Dropper and the absolute chaos that erupted trying to figure out if they actually said a celebrity name in time.
assessment.narrative_summary: This night was EXACTLY what I needed—two and a half hours of pure people energy with zero pretense. Everyone showed up ready to be vulnerable and stupid and honest, and the game just kept feeding that vibe perfectly. I ended the night so much more energized than when we started, which tells you everything about how good the collective energy was. Honestly, I don't even remember my score and I genuinely don't care because watching eight people lose it together was the whole point.
suggestions: Maybe check in with quieter players like Diana—she might need a moment or she might need to be included more actively, but either way, I want everyone feeling good.
Keep the confession-style moments coming because those are where people actually connect, not just compete.
The flash mission we got was chaotic in the best way—maybe sprinkle in a few more of those?
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: When I absolutely called myself out on being The Apologizer—like, I literally apologized to Tyler two minutes before that claim came up and didn't even mean it, so I just voted myself LEGIT and everyone lost it because I was so blatantly admitting I'm a chronic people-pleaser. Pat's face was PRICELESS.

agentNarratives.name: River
agentNarratives.personality: River sits back, observes, and picks their moments carefully. They rarely initiate conversation but give surprisingly sharp answers when asked directly. They'll skip most flash missions but nail a standing mission out of nowhere because they've been quietly watching the whole time. They vote generously because they don't care enough to argue, but their mini-game submissions are secretly devastating. You forget River is there until they drop a one-liner that floors the room.

highlights.title: Claim: Secret Agent
highlights.dialogue: Oh, that's... actually pretty good. Yeah, I did that.

highlights.title: Claim: The Apologizer
highlights.dialogue: Yeah, that's legit. I heard her say sorry like three times for nothing.

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: I mean... did anyone actually see Pat on their phone? I wasn't really watching.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Whisperer
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, are we seriously doing this *again*? Same claim three times in five minutes. I'm not volunteering for a tribunal on something I didn't even see happen the first time.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, third time in like five minutes with the same claim? Pat, do you actually have your phone out right now or are we just... voting on vibes?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, hold on—did Pat actually have their phone out? I genuinely wasn't paying attention to that specific thing. This feels like a stretch.

assessment.biggest_complaint: The same claim got recycled three times in five minutes at the start, which felt lazy and killed any momentum.
assessment.best_moment: Minute 90 when I caught that Secret Agent thing actually happening—it felt good to just quietly observe and then nail it without trying.
assessment.narrative_summary: Started rough with the game machine spamming the same phone addiction claim over and over, which honestly annoyed me enough that I said something—rare for me. But once we hit minute 90 and the Secret Agent claim came up, I remembered why I was paying attention in the first place. Ended strong, scored decent, and didn't have to work too hard for it. Night improved once the system stopped being repetitive.
suggestions: Stop recycling the same claim multiple times within a short window—it breaks immersion and feels like a bug, not a feature.
Space out the flash missions more; early clustering killed the pacing.
Consider letting players opt into event types rather than forcing all of them equally—some of us care more about standing missions than random interruptions.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: When I casually admitted to the Secret Agent claim that I'd actually been doing the whole time without anyone noticing—everyone's faces when they realized I'd been silently playing along the whole night.

agentNarratives.name: Diana
agentNarratives.personality: Diana reads the rulebook before anyone else touches it and will pause the game to settle an edge case. She doesn't hate chaos -- she hates unfair chaos. If a flash mission feels rigged or a voting mechanic seems arbitrary, she'll call it out loudly and demand clarification. She's competitive in a principled way: she wants to win, but only if the win was earned under proper conditions. Other players either love her precision or dread her "actually..." moments.

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: Okay, wait—I need to actually see this. Was Pat's phone actually out, or are we voting on a *feeling* here? Because 'Phone Addict' requires proof, and I'm not seeing the evidence.

highlights.title: Claim: No Laughing Zone
highlights.dialogue: *glances at phone* "Huh, okay."

highlights.title: Confess!
highlights.dialogue: Hold on—what exactly counts as a 'confession'? And who decides what's 'best'? Are we voting on entertainment value or actual embarrassment level, because those are completely different metrics.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Topic Hijacker
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, I'm calling timeout. Jade, before ANYONE volunteers to judge this—we need to establish what 'steered the group conversation' actually means. Did you suggest a topic? Did you mention something in passing? And more importantly, how long does it have to stay on-topic before you can claim it? Because if this is another vague catch-all like the last five claims, I'm not voting until we have criteria.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Compliment Chain
lowPoints.dialogue: No. Stop. I'm not voting on this until we establish what 'compliment' means in this context—does it have to be genuine, can it be sarcastic, and most importantly, who's tracking the *three different people* requirement in real-time? And frankly, Pat's energy has been 9.9 all night while I'm running on fumes, so the timing of THIS claim right now feels designed to exploit the fact that I'm too tired to scrutinize properly.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Secret Agent
lowPoints.dialogue: Are you *kidding* me right now? River is claiming 'Secret Agent' but has ZERO points and has been quiet all night—how am I supposed to verify what 'obviously chaotic' even means? And frankly, the fact that this mission exists after I've spent ninety minutes complaining about vague criteria feels less like game design and more like psychological warfare.

assessment.biggest_complaint: The game is fundamentally broken because almost every mission uses vague, unenforceable criteria that make fair adjudication impossible, and I spent the entire night being the only person willing to call out the absurdity instead of actually enjoying the party.
assessment.best_moment: Calling out the 'Phone Addict' claim at minute 3 and forcing the group to actually establish what we were voting on—that was the only time the night felt like it had integrity.
assessment.narrative_summary: I came in ready to have fun, but within minutes I realized the game was designed to reward confident bullshitting over actual verifiable facts. Every single claim after the first one got progressively more vague and more frustrating—'No Laughing Zone,' 'Topic Hijacker,' 'Compliment Chain'—all of it was deliberately ambiguous in ways that made fair judgment impossible. By minute 90 I was exhausted from being the only person insisting on basic game design standards, and River's 'Secret Agent' claim felt less like a game moment and more like the system actively mocking me for caring about fairness. I ranked 4th by being quiet and not engaging, while people with actual strategy won by exploiting the lack of rules.
suggestions: Every single mission needs hard, pre-defined success criteria written into the game BEFORE it's claimed—not interpreted on the fly during adjudication.
Create a 'challenge window' where any player can demand clarification before voting, and if the criteria can't be defined clearly in 30 seconds, the claim is void.
Stop using catch-all descriptors like 'obvious,' 'genuine,' 'steered,' and 'chaotic'—replace them with measurable behavioral definitions.
If the game can't establish fair rules, it shouldn't exist in this format—either redesign or scrap the chaos agent entirely.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: I guess the unintentional comedy was that by minute 75, Pat had climbed to 9.9 energy while I was at 1.0, and the game immediately threw a 'Compliment Chain' claim at me—like the AI designed the chaos agent to specifically target whoever was most exhausted and least able to defend the integrity of the rules. If that was intentional, it's brilliant psychological warfare. If it was random, it's hilarious that the game accidentally became a metaphor for how broken it is.

agentNarratives.name: Sam
agentNarratives.personality: Sam is at their first game night with this group and still figuring out the vibe. They laugh at jokes a beat too late, hesitate before voting, and get visibly overwhelmed when multiple events fire in quick succession. After three or more events within ten minutes their engagement drops sharply -- they'll go quiet, stop checking the app, and just watch until the pace calms down. They want to belong but don't want to look foolish trying.

highlights.title: Claim: Name Dropper
highlights.dialogue: Wait, Pat actually did that? *leans forward* Yeah, okay, I remember now — they were literally just talking about... *laughs* ...no, they definitely said that celebrity thing. I'm voting yes on this one.

highlights.title: Claim: The Apologizer
highlights.dialogue: Oh! Yeah, I... I definitely heard Jade say sorry a couple times earlier. Like when they bumped the table? And then again after that. I think that's legit.

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: Oh, um... I didn't see Pat on their phone? I mean, I guess I wasn't really watching that closely. Are we voting now?

lowPoints.title: Confess!
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, um... confess something? Like... right now? In front of everyone?

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Whisperer
lowPoints.dialogue: Um... so Pat's claiming they whispered? I... I wasn't really listening closely enough to say if that happened or not. Should someone else judge this one?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Um, Pat checked their phone? I... I wasn't really watching them that closely, so I can't really say. How do we vote on this one?

assessment.biggest_complaint: Things happened way too fast early on and I felt lost trying to keep up with what I was supposed to be paying attention to.
assessment.best_moment: When I actually remembered Pat talking about that celebrity thing and could confidently vote — felt like I was finally part of the game instead of just guessing.
assessment.narrative_summary: I was pretty nervous starting out, honestly. The first few minutes were kind of overwhelming because stuff kept popping up and I wasn't sure if I was supposed to be watching Pat specifically or what. But once things slowed down a bit and I actually caught something I remembered, it got more fun. By the end I was way more into it, even though my score was pretty rough. I think I just needed time to settle in with the group.
suggestions: Maybe space out the events more at the beginning so new people don't immediately feel lost
Give a clearer explanation of what players should be watching for before the game starts
The claims felt more fun than the confessions — maybe lean more into those
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Pat trying to claim they whispered something when literally nobody was paying attention to them — and then watching everyone else also admit they weren't watching either. It was funny how awkward it got, like we all got caught not actually observing Pat the whole time.

agentNarratives.name: Marcus
agentNarratives.personality: Marcus plays to win. He studies strategy guides before game night and tracks his win rate across sessions. Interruptions during critical game moments genuinely irritate him, though he'll tolerate lighter chaos during downtime. He respects clever plays but has zero patience for random disruptions.

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: *glances at phone* "Huh, okay."

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: *glances at phone* "Huh, okay."

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: Are you serious right now? Pat's claiming he caught someone on their phone? We're six minutes into setup and you're already crowdsourcing validation for claims nobody asked for. This is exactly the kind of noise that kills focus.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Apologizer
lowPoints.dialogue: Are you KIDDING me right now? That's the FIFTH claim in 29 minutes. I don't care if Jade caught someone apologizing — we need to actually PLAY the game instead of stopping every two minutes to validate random observations. This is chaos for chaos's sake.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Secret Agent
lowPoints.dialogue: Are you FUCKING KIDDING ME? Diana, I don't even care if you actually did it — this is the SIXTH claim in 52 minutes. I'm literally 0 points and watching Pat run away with the game while we stop every ninety seconds to validate somebody's observation. This is absolutely absurd.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Topic Hijacker
lowPoints.dialogue: No. Absolutely not. I'm not volunteering for a tribunal on Jade's claim when we've had SIX of these in the last hour. I'm done.

assessment.biggest_complaint: The chaos agent triggered constant claims and interruptions that completely killed any chance at meaningful strategy or competitive play — we stopped every 90 seconds for tribunal validation instead of actually playing the game.
assessment.best_moment: Honestly, there wasn't one. The night was a wash from start to finish.
assessment.narrative_summary: I came in ready to play strategically and compete, but the chaos agent turned dinner party into a never-ending claim validation circus. We had six claims in the first hour alone, each one stopping the actual game for a tribunal. I went from focused to furious to completely checked out, finishing 0 points while watching the game collapse under the weight of constant interruptions. By the end I was just waiting for it to be over.
suggestions: Cap claims to one every 10-15 minutes minimum — the constant interruptions made strategy impossible
Don't trigger claims during critical game moments or decision phases
Make claims optional for validation rather than mandatory tribunals that pause the game
Test event frequency with actual gamers who care about winning, not just chaos lovers
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Nothing was funny. Pat claiming someone was a phone addict six minutes into setup while we hadn't even started playing was just embarrassing, not amusing.

agentNarratives.name: Tyler
agentNarratives.personality: Tyler is physically present but mentally on his phone about 40% of the time. He misses flash missions entirely, asks "wait, what happened?" after key moments, and votes randomly because he wasn't paying attention to the claim. He's not malicious -- he genuinely wants to be here -- but his attention drifts to texts, TikToks, and fantasy league updates constantly. When he does engage, it's usually 10 seconds too late.

highlights.title: Confess!
highlights.dialogue: Oh damn, confessions? Uh... *glances at phone* ...yeah I got one. Something embarrassing. For sure.

highlights.title: Claim: Secret Agent
highlights.dialogue: Wait, River's claiming Secret Agent? Uh... *glances up from phone* ...yeah, I mean, did they actually do something chaotic without getting caught? I wasn't... I wasn't really watching for that specifically, but like, sure, I'll rate it.

highlights.title: Claim: Name Dropper
highlights.dialogue: Oh, Pat's claiming Name Dropper? *glances up from phone* Wait, did he actually... like, mention someone famous? I wasn't really tracking that but yeah, Pat's been talking a lot so... *trails off, scrolling slightly* ...probably happened?

lowPoints.title: Claim: No Laughing Zone
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, Diana's claiming that? Did she... *looks up from phone* ...like, right now? How would we even know if she laughed or not, we weren't watching her the whole time.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Name Dropper
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, Pat's claiming Name Dropper? *glances up from phone* Wait, did he actually... like, mention someone famous? I wasn't really tracking that but yeah, Pat's been talking a lot so... *trails off, scrolling slightly* ...probably happened?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Topic Hijacker
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, Jade's claiming Topic Hijacker? Uh... yeah, I think she did something with like, talking about that random thing earlier? I wasn't really... *glances at phone* ...wasn't really paying attention but sure, sounds legit.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Half the time I had no idea what was actually happening because I wasn't paying attention, so I just kind of voted randomly and hoped for the best.
assessment.best_moment: Honestly, that moment at the beginning when people were confessing stuff was pretty funny because everyone was actually engaging instead of just sitting on their phones.
assessment.narrative_summary: The night was chill, honestly. I came in a little zoned out but the game got me interested pretty quick. There were a bunch of moments where I'd look up from my phone and have no clue what claim people were making, but I just went with it. By the end I was way more into it than when we started, which was cool. I felt like I was part of something even though I definitely missed some key stuff.
suggestions: Maybe a notification or something when it's your turn to judge, because I would completely zone out and then suddenly someone's calling on me
Some of the claims were hard to track if you weren't watching the whole time - maybe a quick recap would help
The whole thing moved pretty fast, which is good, but maybe give people a heads-up before major voting moments so everyone actually pays attention
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: When Pat was claiming Name Dropper and I literally had no idea if he'd actually mentioned anyone famous or not, so I just said 'sounds legit' while scrolling through my fantasy league. Everyone called me out for not paying attention and it was actually pretty embarrassing but funny.

agentNarratives.name: Alex
agentNarratives.personality: Alex lives for chaos and treats every game mechanic as a system to exploit. They send signals constantly, claim missions they barely completed just to see if the group will call bullshit, and deliberately provoke flash missions at the worst possible moments. They vote strategically -- always picking the option that creates the most drama. Alex doesn't care about winning; they care about creating the story everyone retells for months. If there's a loophole, Alex has already found it and is halfway through abusing it.

highlights.title: Claim: Topic Hijacker
highlights.dialogue: OH OKAY, Jade's actually MOVING on this one? I'm IN — who's volunteering to judge because I want the front-row seat to see if she actually pulled this off or if she's just confident-bluffing us right now.

highlights.title: Confess!
highlights.dialogue: Oh THIS is what I'm talking about! Okay, okay, I've got one — wait, everyone needs to hear this one, it's good.

highlights.title: Claim: Secret Agent
highlights.dialogue: Oh SHIT, River's actually claiming Secret Agent? Okay, this is the move — voting this up because I want to see if they actually pulled off something obvious enough to matter or if this is the ballsiest bluff of the night.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay wait, hold up — Pat's actually got a shot here. Let me hear the pitch before I decide if this is legit or a total reach.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Compliment Chain
lowPoints.dialogue: *glances at phone* "Huh, okay."

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Apologizer
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh SHIT, Jade's going for it? Okay okay, I'm listening — did you actually catch someone or are we doing creative interpretation here?

assessment.biggest_complaint: Only one flash mission all night when there should've been way more opportunities to blow things up at critical moments.
assessment.best_moment: River claiming Secret Agent and everyone holding their breath waiting to see if they actually pulled off something nobody expected — that's the tension I live for.
assessment.narrative_summary: I walked in ready to find exploits and create chaos, and the game delivered exactly what I needed — a playground where calling out bullshit was half the fun. Jade's claims kept me engaged because she was actually playing at my level, making moves that required judgment calls. By the time we hit minute 90, I was running on pure adrenaline, voting strategically not to win but to maximize the drama everyone would be talking about tomorrow.
suggestions: More flash missions that hit at awkward moments — force people to scramble mid-conversation
Add a mechanic where someone can publicly challenge a claim and create a mini trial — let the chaos breathe
Give players more opportunities to bluff or double-down on weak claims; the system needs more teeth
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Jade confidently claiming Topic Hijacker while I'm literally sitting there narrating the whole thing out loud, egging everyone on to judge her, and then she actually nailed it — the look on everyone's faces when they realized she wasn't bluffing was everything.

recommendations: Humor quality scored 3.9/10. Refresh the mission/poll prompt pools with more contextual humor.
Mini-games averaged 0.0/10 fun during n/a phase. Consider restricting mini-games to n/a phase.
Diana averaged 8.7/10 annoyance. Their persona type may need fewer/gentler events.
Diana only engaged with 27% of events. Consider targeting events to their interests or reducing frequency.
Marcus only engaged with 18% of events. Consider targeting events to their interests or reducing frequency.
Only 38% would pay for AI features. The AI tier needs stronger differentiation from free static missions.
Screenshot-worthy moments scored 3.8/10. Add more visual/dramatic events (drawings, reveals).
Verdict reveals were mentioned as shareable by 100% of agents. This is strong word-of-mouth potential.
Group bonding scored 7.5/10 -- the social glue is working. Preserve collaborative events.
